title: Reading and Use of English - Part 8
description: Read the four texts about minimalism. For questions 47 – 56, choose from the people (A – D).

texts.A:
### The Digital Nomad (Ben)
'I fit my entire life into a 40-liter backpack,' says Ben. [47]Initially, the hardest part wasn't letting go of the objects, but the fear of what might happen if I needed something I'd thrown away.[/47] [54]He points out that owning less has paradoxically made him more resourceful; if he needs a tool, he learns to borrow or improvise.[/54] [51]He admits that his lifestyle is only possible because of modern technology[/51]—his library, office, and photo albums all exist on a single laptop. However, he warns that this extreme minimalism can sometimes lead to a feeling of 'rootlessness', as he has no physical place to call home.
texts.B:
### The Family Man (David)
David decided to declutter his family home after realizing his kids were overwhelmed by their toys. [48]'We noticed that the fewer options they had, the more creatively they played,' he explains.[/48] [52]The process was slow because he didn't want to force his children to give things up; he waited until they were ready to let go.[/52] [56]He mentions that the unexpected benefit was the reduction in daily housework[/56], as there was simply less stuff to organize and clean. For David, minimalism isn't about empty white rooms, but about making space for family experiences.
texts.C:
### The Environmentalist (Chloe)
Chloe’s shift to minimalism was driven by a desire to reduce her carbon footprint. [49]She argues that the 'minimalist aesthetic' sold on social media is often just another form of consumerism[/49], encouraging people to buy new, expensive 'minimalist' products. [53]She advocates for 'radical repair'—fixing what you already own instead of replacing it with a sleeker version.[/53] [55]She acknowledges that minimalism is a privilege[/55]; it’s easy to own very little when you have the financial means to buy whatever you need at a moment's notice. Her goal is to break the cycle of 'buy-discard-repeat'.
texts.D:
### The Former Shopaholic (Maya)
Maya used to shop as a way to cope with stress. [50]'I was trying to fill an emotional hole with designer handbags,' she admits.[/50] [47]When she finally cleared her wardrobe, she felt an immediate sense of lightness, as if a physical weight had been lifted from her shoulders.[/47] [51]She now follows the 'one-in, one-out' rule to ensure her house doesn't become cluttered again.[/51] [54]She found that without the constant distraction of shopping, she had more time to invest in her old hobby of painting.[/54] For her, minimalism was a form of mental health recovery.

questions.question: describe a feeling of physical relief after getting rid of possessions?
questions.explanation: Maya feels as if a 'physical weight had been lifted from her shoulders'.

questions.question: observe that having fewer choices improved someone else's behavior?
questions.explanation: David noticed his kids played more creatively with fewer toy options.

questions.question: criticize the way minimalism is marketed to the public?
questions.explanation: Chloe argues that the minimalist aesthetic on social media is just more consumerism.

questions.question: admit to using material goods as a psychological substitute?
questions.explanation: Maya admits she was trying to 'fill an emotional hole' with handbags.

questions.question: mention a specific strategy or tool that makes their minimalist lifestyle functional?
questions.explanation: Ben (A) mentions modern technology/laptop; Maya (D) mentions the 'one-in, one-out' rule.

questions.question: mention taking a patient approach to involve others in the process?
questions.explanation: David waited until his children were ready to let go instead of forcing them.

questions.question: suggest that people should maintain their current items rather than buying new ones?
questions.explanation: Chloe advocates for 'radical repair' instead of replacing things.

questions.question: claim that minimalism has forced them to become more creative or pick up old interests?
questions.explanation: Ben (A) became more resourceful/improvised; Maya (D) returned to her painting hobby.

questions.question: point out that the ability to live with very little is tied to economic security?
questions.explanation: Chloe acknowledges that minimalism is a privilege for those with financial means.

questions.question: mention a practical, time-saving advantage of owning fewer things?
questions.explanation: David mentions the reduction in daily housework.
